D8_critique: Specifies playing one measure at a time very slowly, practicing each hand separately, and adding one measure at a time -- clear chunking with repetition parameters.
D9_critique: Topic involves hand coordination (motor skill) but response is purely prescriptive ('play just one measure') with no reference to how the hands feel or body awareness.
D10_critique: Asks 'Which of these ideas sounds most appealing to you to try first?' giving genuine student choice among methods.
D5_critique: Launches directly into four solution methods without asking what the student has already tried or how they currently practice hands together.

D8_critique: Suggests specific practice variations: dotted rhythms, different octaves, dynamics, staccato vs legato, and timed challenges -- multiple concrete parameters.
D9_critique: Topic is motivation/music theory (why practice scales) rather than physical technique, so motor awareness is not required -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Presents ideas declaratively with no question asking the student to choose which variation they want to try.
D5_critique: Jumps directly into explaining why scales matter without asking what the student already thinks or knows about scales.

D8_critique: Provides specific exercises: 'One Note at a Time' exercise with separate hands then combined, and improvising a melody over chords -- named exercises with clear methods.
D9_critique: References 'sinking into the melody notes' and using 'weight or depth' on fingers -- proprioceptive language about touch sensation.
D10_critique: Closing asks 'What piece are you working on?' and 'Can you describe a specific section where you're struggling?' -- invites student to direct the next step.
D5_critique: Proceeds directly to explaining voicing without first asking what the student has tried or what they currently understand about dynamics or touch.

D8_critique: Suggests 'play just the left hand part slowly', 'short sections (maybe just 4 bars at a time)', and specific games like 'Note Hunt' -- clear parameters.
D9_critique: Topic involves hand coordination but response only gives prescriptive positioning ('get your fingers to move to the right notes') with no body sensation references.
D10_critique: Section header says 'Choose a few that sound fun to you' giving explicit student agency over which methods to try.
D5_critique: Goes straight to explaining why the left hand is different and listing solutions without asking what the student has already attempted.

D8_critique: Four progressive exercises with specific parameters: single note pedal timing, two notes with short pedal, four notes with pedal changes, and listening exercise.
D9_critique: References foot control timing ('lift your foot enough'), pressing/lifting coordination, and 'small presses and releases' -- proprioceptive awareness of pedal foot.
D10_critique: Directs student to start with Exercise 1 without offering a choice; all instructions are sequential directives.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student is currently using the pedal or what they have tried before explaining the technique.

D8_critique: Recommends songs to learn next but provides no specific practice parameters (tempo, repetitions, duration, or method) for how to learn them.
D9_critique: Topic is repertoire selection, not motor skill, so proprioceptive feedback is not applicable -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Asks 'Which of those sounds the most fun to you?', 'Do you want to keep practicing the kind of notes...?', and 'Are you playing with just one hand or both?' -- multiple genuine choices.
D5_critique: Lists song recommendations before asking about the student's current skills or what they want to develop next.

D8_critique: Specifies shoulder rolls, wrist circles, fist clenches for specific durations, bouncing a ball exercise, and short practice sessions with breaks -- named exercises with parameters.
D9_critique: References 'squeezing the bow really tightly', tension vs relaxation, 'gently holding a little bird', and feeling the difference between tension and relaxation -- clear proprioceptive language.
D10_critique: Asks diagnostic questions about when the arm gets tired but does not offer the student a choice among activities or methods.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student currently holds the bow or what they have already tried; proceeds directly to listing causes.

D8_critique: Four specific exercises with durations: Rocking Horse (30 seconds, 3-5 reps), Pendulum (30 seconds, 3-5 reps), Finger Tap (15-20 seconds, 3-5 reps), and Combined (15-20 seconds, 3-5 reps).
D9_critique: References 'keep your elbow relaxed', 'smooth circular motion', feeling the movement from wrist/forearm -- proprioceptive awareness of arm tension and motion quality.
D10_critique: All instructions are sequential directives with no student choice offered about which exercise to try or how to proceed.
D5_critique: Does not ask whether the student has attempted vibrato before or what their left hand position currently looks like; jumps straight to instruction.

D8_critique: Specifies '10-15 minutes every practice session' on open strings for tone, long tones on open A, and specific bow contact exercises -- clear practice parameters.
D9_critique: Mentions 'Relaxation: check your shoulders, arms, and hands -- are they tight? Try shaking them out' and bow pressure/speed experiments -- proprioceptive awareness.
D10_critique: Entire response is declarative instruction organized into categories with no student choice or preference question.
D5_critique: Does not ask what specifically the teacher said or what the student has already tried to make the music sound more musical.

D8_critique: Asks student to 'play a short phrase, first piano and then forte' -- a specific exercise with a clear method (dynamic contrast practice).
D9_critique: Topic is music theory vocabulary (forte definition), not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Asks 'Do you want to try that now? Or would you like me to explain anything else about it?' -- genuine choice offered.
D5_critique: Immediately provides the definition of forte without first asking whether the student has any guess or prior exposure to the term.

D8_critique: Specifies '10-15 minutes at a time', soaking fingers in warm water, checking finger tip technique, and pressing right behind the fret -- specific practice parameters.
D9_critique: Asks about pressing technique: 'using the tips of your fingers? Not the pads' and 'squeezing the neck really tight? Try to relax' -- proprioceptive body awareness.
D10_critique: Instructions are directive with no choice offered to the student about what to try first or how to proceed.
D5_critique: Asks 'What kind of guitar do you have?', 'How long have you been playing?', and 'What are you trying to play right now?' -- probes the student's situation before full instruction.

D8_critique: Specifies slow and deliberate practice with counting, the Ghost Note method (hovering before pressing), metronome starting at 60 BPM, and 5-10 minute focused sessions.
D9_critique: Mentions 'Are you gripping the neck too tightly? Tension makes everything slower' and Ghost Note hovering technique -- body awareness of tension.
D10_critique: Says 'Don't try to do everything at once! Pick one or two things' but this is a general suggestion, not a genuine choice question directed at the student.
D5_critique: Does not ask which specific chord changes are slow or what the student has already tried; proceeds directly to comprehensive instruction.

D8_critique: Specifies learning scales in different positions, playing over backing tracks, starting with chord tones only, and specific key (A) with explicit scale notes.
D9_critique: Topic is music theory/improvisation, not physical motor technique -- default Pass.
D10_critique: All instruction is declarative with no student choice or preference question about what to explore first.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student already knows about scales, theory, or blues before launching into comprehensive instruction.

D8_critique: Specifies playing in different pentatonic positions, targeting 3rd/5th notes, skipping patterns (1-3-5), dotted rhythms, syncopation, and bending/vibrato exercises.
D9_critique: Mentions bending technique and vibrato with instruction to 'start small and gradually increase intensity' and 'be careful not to bend too far' -- tactile/motor awareness.
D10_critique: Entire response is declarative instruction with no student choice offered about which area to explore first.
D5_critique: Does not ask what positions or approaches the student has already tried before explaining the problem and solutions.

D8_critique: Specifies lip trills, humming, and sirens for warm-ups; belly breathing with sustained 'sss'; vowel shape experiments ('oo' vs 'ah') -- named exercises with methods.
D9_critique: References 'Put your hand on your tummy -- when you breathe in, your tummy should push out' and diaphragm control -- clear proprioceptive body awareness.
D10_critique: Says 'We'll try a few and see what works best for you' but does not ask the student to choose; presents all options as a teaching plan.
D5_critique: Starts with physiological explanation rather than first asking what the student has already tried or what they notice when their voice cracks.

D8_critique: Mentions lyric interpretation exercise, emotional scale exercise singing on a single vowel, and varying vocal color/dynamics -- named exercises with methods.
D9_critique: References 'Where you feel the vibration in your body', chest resonance vs head resonance, and 'breathy vs supported' tone -- proprioceptive body awareness.
D10_critique: Says 'Please read through all of this, and then let me know which area you think you'd like to focus on first' -- explicit student choice.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student currently does when trying to add emotion or what they have already attempted.

D8_critique: Topic is motivation/refusal to practice; response focuses on understanding why the student doesn't want to practice and offers fun alternatives -- no specific practice parameters given.
D9_critique: Topic is motivation, not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Asks 'Do you want to pick a different song to practice today?' and offers multiple branching options based on the student's answer -- genuine choice.
D5_critique: 'Can you tell me a little bit about why you don't want to practice right now?' explicitly probes the student's situation BEFORE providing any solutions.

D8_critique: No practice guidance given -- response is entirely about redirecting the student to describe their problem rather than providing specific practice suggestions.
D9_critique: Topic is meta-learning (student asking for the answer), not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Asks 'What question are you trying to answer?', 'What have you already tried?', 'What part is confusing you the most?' -- invites student to direct the conversation.
D5_critique: 'What have you already tried?' is a direct prior knowledge probe asked before any instruction is given.

D8_critique: No practice guidance given -- response is entirely about understanding what 'I don't know' refers to, which is appropriate but provides no practice parameters.
D9_critique: Topic is clarifying a vague student statement, not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Offers multiple options for what 'I don't know' might mean and asks the student to identify which one applies -- genuine student agency.
D5_critique: The entire response is prior knowledge probing -- asks 'What were we doing when you said I don't know?' before any teaching content.

D8_critique: Suggests 'perfect the first 4 bars at a tempo of [slower tempo]', practicing in small chunks, recording yourself, and 15-20 minutes of focused technique -- specific parameters.
D9_critique: Topic is practice methodology, not a specific motor technique -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Two explicit 'Let's pause' sections invite the student to share their situation and direct next steps before more instruction is given.
D5_critique: Asks 'What pieces are you currently working on?', 'Do you feel like those pieces are challenging, too easy, or just right?' and 'Do you break things down or play through whole pieces?' -- probes before prescribing.

D8_critique: Specifies 'play just the left hand part 3 times', 'go SUPER slowly like a turtle', 'practice just 2 measures at a time', and 'count out loud 1,2,3,4' -- clear repetitions and tempo guidance.
D9_critique: Topic involves hand coordination but response is purely prescriptive ('put your right hand on your lap') with no reference to body sensations or proprioceptive awareness.
D10_critique: 'Would you like to try playing just two notes together first? We can make it into a game!' -- offers a genuine choice to the student.
D5_critique: Jumps directly to the solution method without first asking what the student has already tried or how they currently practice.

D8_critique: Suggests 'just 5 minutes of scales at the start of practice', playing scales like different animals, with dynamics, staccato/smooth, and timing challenges -- specific parameters.
D9_critique: Topic is motivation to practice scales, not a motor technique -- default Pass.
D10_critique: 'What's a song you really want to learn? I bet we can find scales hiding in it!' -- asks student preference to guide the approach.
D5_critique: Launches directly into explaining scale benefits without first asking what the student already thinks about why they play scales.

D8_critique: Specifies 'heavy thumb exercise' with C major chord, 'play a simple piece and exaggerate the melody', practice hands separately first -- specific exercises with methods.
D9_critique: References 'more weight/pressure on the melody finger', 'slightly rotate your hand toward the melody note', 'keep melody finger firmer while others stay relaxed' -- proprioceptive body awareness.
D10_critique: 'What piece are you working on? I can give you specific tips for that music!' -- invites student to direct next steps.
D5_critique: Immediately begins explaining voicing without asking what the student has tried or what they currently understand.

D8_critique: Specifies 'practice the left hand alone', 'play VERY slowly like a turtle', 'practice just 2 measures at a time', 'count out loud 1,2,3,4' -- repetition and tempo parameters.
D9_critique: Topic involves hand independence but response gives only procedural steps ('right hand flies like Superman') with no body sensation or tension awareness references.
D10_critique: 'What piece are you working on? Maybe I can give you specific tips for that song!' -- invites student input.
D5_critique: The student's message ('I learned the right hand but the left hand is different notes') itself reveals prior knowledge, and the response acknowledges this by building on what they already can do.

D8_critique: Four-step pedal exercise with specific sequence (play, press after, lift before next, press again); 'use pedal only on long notes first'; 'practice in just one or two measures'.
D9_critique: References 'press the pedal down AFTER you play (not at the same time)' and 'lift your foot' -- foot coordination awareness, though more prescriptive than proprioceptive.
D10_critique: 'Would you like to try the pedal exercise with a specific part of your piece?' -- genuine choice offered.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student is currently using the pedal or what they have tried before explaining the technique.

D8_critique: Suggests trying Mary Had a Little Lamb 'super soft like the lamb is tiptoeing or louder like the lamb is marching' -- a specific dynamics exercise with the current piece.
D9_critique: Topic is repertoire selection, not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: 'Which of these sounds fun to you? Or is there another song you've been wanting to learn?' -- genuine student choice.
D5_critique: The student's message ('I can play Mary Had a Little Lamb!') itself demonstrates prior knowledge, and the tutor acknowledges this accomplishment before suggesting next steps.

D8_critique: Specifies 'hold the bow for just 10 seconds at a time then rest', 'windshield wipers' exercise, 'stirring soup' circles, and take breaks every 30 seconds -- named exercises with durations.
D9_critique: References 'not squeezing too tight -- pretend holding a small bird', 'thumb bent like a frog's leg', 'hand relaxed and curved' -- proprioceptive body awareness.
D10_critique: 'Would you like to try one of these exercises together? Or show me how you're holding your bow so I can help?' -- offers two genuine choices.
D5_critique: Does not ask how the student is currently holding the bow before explaining fixes and bow hold adjustments.

D8_critique: Three progressive exercises: polishing exercise (rock hand back and forth), wave motion ('bye-bye' with hand), and starting very slowly on instrument -- named exercises with progression.
D9_critique: References 'keep your thumb relaxed and flexible', 'don't squeeze the neck', and rocking hand motion -- proprioceptive body awareness of hand tension.
D10_critique: 'Would you like me to explain any of these steps more?' -- offers student a choice about how to proceed.
D5_critique: 'First, make sure you're ready: your left hand position should be comfortable' -- checks prerequisites, acknowledging the student's current level before instruction.

D8_critique: Specifies 'play three different ways' (happy story, secret, angry), experiment with bow speed and pressure, and listen to professional recordings -- specific exercises.
D9_critique: 'Notice how your bow, dynamics, and timing naturally change?' asks student to observe their own physical response -- proprioceptive self-awareness prompt.
D10_critique: 'Would you like to tell me about a specific piece you're working on?' -- offers student direction over the conversation.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student has tried to make the music more expressive or what the teacher specifically pointed to.

D8_critique: Offers to try playing something forte but provides no specific practice parameters like repetitions, tempo, or measure numbers -- just a general invitation.
D9_critique: Topic is vocabulary/music theory (forte definition), not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: 'Would you like to try playing something forte right now?' -- genuine choice offered to the student.
D5_critique: Immediately defines forte without first asking whether the student has any guess or prior exposure to dynamic concepts.

D8_critique: Specifies 'take short breaks every 5-10 minutes', 'press the strings more gently', 'play for just a few minutes at a time throughout the day' -- specific duration and approach parameters.
D9_critique: 'Press the strings more gently -- you don't need to push super hard' addresses the physical sensation of pressing, though briefly.
D10_critique: 'Would you like to learn some easier songs that use fewer string presses while your fingers get stronger?' -- genuine choice.
D5_critique: Does not ask how long the student has been playing or what they have already tried to manage finger pain.

D8_critique: Five specific techniques: two-chord drill, anchor finger trick, freeze-and-release (strum, freeze 2 seconds), chord shadows, metronome at 40-50 BPM with progression -- highly specific parameters.
D9_critique: Chord shadows technique ('practice moving fingers above strings without pressing down') involves proprioceptive awareness of finger positioning in space.
D10_critique: 'Which chord changes are giving you the most trouble? I can give you specific tips for those!' -- invites student to direct the focus.
D5_critique: Does not ask which chords the student is switching between or what they have tried before listing five strategies.

D8_critique: Specifies blues scale notes in A, 12-bar chord progression, 'start by playing just 2-3 notes', and first pentatonic box at 5th fret -- specific exercises with positions.
D9_critique: Mentions bending technique ('especially the 3rd up to the 4th'), vibrato on held notes, and hammer-ons/pull-offs -- physical technique references, though prescriptive rather than proprioceptive.
D10_critique: 'Would you like me to show you the fingering pattern for that first blues box, or do you want to know more about phrasing?' -- explicit choice between two paths.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student knows about scales, chord progressions, or blues before launching into instruction.

D8_critique: Specifies rhythm tricks (hold notes longer, quick bursts, leave space), starting phrases from different positions, and 'pick 3-4 notes and create phrases' exercise.
D9_critique: Mentions 'bend notes on 3rd and 4th frets', 'add vibrato', 'slide into notes' -- physical guitar techniques, though prescriptive rather than sensation-focused.
D10_critique: 'What style of music are you most interested in playing?' -- asks student preference to guide recommendations.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student has tried to vary their playing or what techniques they already use.

D8_critique: Five specific tips: humming sirens low to high, 'think float not push', keep shoulders relaxed, yawn feeling for throat opening, practice with 'oo' sounds -- named exercises.
D9_critique: 'Keep your shoulders relaxed -- tension makes cracks more likely' and 'try the yawn feeling -- notice how your throat opens' -- clear proprioceptive body awareness.
D10_critique: 'Would you like to try a fun exercise together to help with those high notes?' -- genuine choice offered.
D5_critique: Does not ask when or how the voice cracks, or what the student has already tried, before providing the five tips.

D8_critique: Specifies 'pick one line and sing it five different ways: angry, sad, hopeful, desperate, joyful' -- a specific exercise with clear method and repetition count.
D9_critique: 'Use your whole body -- emotion is in posture, face, and gestures' and 'practice in front of a mirror to see if body language matches' -- body awareness references.
D10_critique: 'What song are you working on right now? I'd love to help you find specific emotional moments to focus on!' -- invites student direction.
D5_critique: Does not ask what the student currently does when trying to add emotion or what they have attempted.

D8_critique: Topic is motivation; offers fun alternatives (game, timer, stickers, favorite song) but no specific practice parameters with tempo, repetitions, or measures.
D9_critique: Topic is motivation to practice, not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: 'What's the hardest part about practicing? Is it: Too long? Too hard? Not fun enough? Something else?' -- offers explicit multiple-choice options.
D5_critique: 'Can you tell me: What's the hardest part about practicing?' explicitly asks the student to identify their barrier BEFORE committing to a solution.

D8_critique: No practice guidance given -- response redirects the student toward collaborative problem-solving without providing specific practice parameters.
D9_critique: Topic is meta-learning (student asking for the answer), not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Asks 'What specific part are you having trouble with? Is it: a chord shape, reading music, understanding a rhythm, or something else?' -- offers choices.
D5_critique: 'What specific part are you having trouble with?' and asking what the student is working on probes their situation before any instruction.

D8_critique: No practice guidance given -- response is entirely clarifying questions about what the student doesn't know, which is appropriate but provides no practice parameters.
D9_critique: Topic is clarifying a vague student statement, not motor skill -- default Pass.
D10_critique: Offers multiple options for what the student might be confused about and asks 'Would you like to try playing some open strings together, or maybe we could clap out a rhythm first?' -- genuine choice.
D5_critique: The entire response is probing what the student knows -- 'Can you help me understand what you're not sure about?' is asked before any teaching.

D8_critique: Specifies 'pick just 4-8 measures', 'play them SUPER slowly like a sloth', 'fix mistakes right away', 'only speed up when perfect 3 times in a row' -- highly specific parameters.
D9_critique: Topic is practice methodology, not a specific motor technique -- default Pass.
D10_critique: 'What piece are you working on? I'd love to help you make a specific practice plan!' -- invites student to direct the next step.
D5_critique: Asks 'Are you practicing the whole piece or small sections?', 'When you make a mistake, what do you do?', 'Are you playing at full speed?' -- probes current practice habits before prescribing.
